titleStmt.title: The Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive

respStmt.orgName: University of South Carolina Provost Humanities Grants
University of South Carolina Department of English
Center for Digital Humanities at the University of South Carolina
Magellan Scholar Program at the University of South Carolina
National Society of the Daughters of the American Revolution
Carnegie-Whitney Grant from the American Library Association

availability.p: Periodicals and books published before 1 January 1923 are in the public domain. If re-publishing, quoting from, or reproducing images from this digital archive, we request that you cite it as follows: Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive, Center for Digital Humanities, University of South Carolina.

Periodicals and books published on or after 1 January 1923 may be protected by U.S. Copyright Law. All attempts to secure permission for republishing post-1923 material in this archive have been made. The names, logos, and identifying images, as well as the content of the articles, remain the property of the periodicals and/or of the authors and artists themselves. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder. Archival material published in this archive is used with permission of the institution or library that holds it. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder.
Responsibility for any use of the contents of this digital archive rests exclusively with the user.

div.p: The most weird story we have heard is told by two well-known men of this place, who were on a prospecting tour some three weeks since, near Cobblestone mountain, at the northern boundary of this county. One cold night they were simultaneously awakened about 2 o'clock by the noise of crackling brush that had been thrown on the fire. They arose to a sitting posture and saw the figure of an Indian woman standing by the fire. She was dressed in a robe of gayly colored material that nearly reached ther feet. A glistening necklace, evidently of gold and silver, enriched her neck, and hanging pendant from this was a number of bears' claws. Her black hair reached below her waist. In her ears were large hoop earrings of gold.

Upon seeing the form one of the young men instinctively reached for his rifle by his side. When the figure saw the motion she motioned for him not to fire and moved down the trail, beckoning to them. Before disappearing from view she again beckoned, but they were too dumbfounded to follow. The next morning they followed the trail, and after much difficulty traced the footprints to the base of a high cliff about a mile from their camp. The rest of the story told is to this effect: "The next morning we discussed the matter and determined to investigate. So the next night we took our blankets and went to the base of the cliff. At about midnight, the same hour, the figure appeared to us; we saw a bright phosphorescent light on the brow of the cliff, and I am sure we heard a voice calling 'Meeneeah! Meeneeah!'"

An old Indian tradition is to the effect that many, many years ago an Indian maiden—Meeneeah, the only daughter of a chief—was lost in this region and starved to death near the place called Squaw Flat. It is said that many camping parties have seen the phosphorescent light spoken of in the vicinity where these young men were camped.
